Embedded Finance Is Eating Software — Here's the Stack Behind It
Every app wants to be a bank now. Stripe, Unit, and the API economy have made that disturbingly easy. Here's what's under the hood.

There's a pattern worth noticing: the most-used financial products in your life probably aren't made by a bank.

Your Swiggy wallet. Ola Money. The EMI option that appeared when you checked out on Flipkart. The credit card that's actually just a Slice card wrapped in a different brand. These are embedded finance products — and the infrastructure that makes them possible has gotten disturbingly easy to wire up.

## What "Embedded Finance" Actually Means

Embedded finance is when a non-financial company adds financial services to its product. Not linking to a bank — actually offering the service itself, inside the experience.

The classic example is Uber offering its drivers instant pay-outs to a branded debit card. Uber isn't a bank. But it acts like one at the point of need, and its users never leave the app.

The key technical enabler is Banking as a Service (BaaS) — a layer of APIs that let any company plug into regulated banking infrastructure without becoming a bank themselves.

## The Stack

Here's roughly how the layers work:

**Regulated bank at the bottom** — Someone has to actually hold the deposits and carry the banking license. In India, this might be an RBI-licensed bank like SBM or Suryoday. In the US, it's often Cross River or Evolve.

**BaaS middleware** — Companies like Unit (US), Razorpay (India), or Setu sit between the regulated bank and the end-product. They abstract the messy compliance and core banking systems into clean REST APIs.

**Your product on top** — You call the API. You create a wallet, issue a virtual card, initiate a payout. You own the user experience. The BaaS layer and bank handle the rest.

## Why This Is a Big Deal

The marginal cost of adding a wallet to a consumer app dropped by roughly 90% between 2018 and 2023. What used to require a banking license, a compliance team, and a custom integration with a core banking system now takes a few API calls and a signed BaaS agreement.

This means every startup with a captive user base is now asking: should we also be their bank?

The answer is often yes, because financial services have dramatically higher margins than the core product. A food delivery company makes 4–6% margins on food. A lending product layered on top of that transaction data can make 15–20%.

## What Could Go Wrong

The regulatory arbitrage that makes this work is not permanent. The RBI has already tightened prepaid payment instrument rules once. The CFPB in the US is actively scrutinizing BaaS arrangements.

The embedded finance stack is efficient — until the regulated bank at the bottom decides the reputational risk isn't worth the revenue, as happened when several US banks pulled out of BaaS partnerships in 2023.

The infrastructure is real and powerful. The regulatory moat is not as wide as most founders assume.

Why This Is a Big Deal
Why India's UPI Is the Blueprint Every Central Bank Is Secretly Copying
Real-time payments aren't just convenient — they're a geopolitical flex. Here's what the rest of the world is quietly learning from India's experiment.

Real-time payments have quietly become a geopolitical instrument. And India's Unified Payments Interface sits right at the center of that shift.

## What UPI Actually Is

Most people use UPI every day without thinking about what it's doing under the hood. When you pay at a kirana store by scanning a QR code, you're touching a layer of infrastructure that processes over 13 billion transactions a month — and does it for free.

The architecture is deceptively simple: a common interoperability layer sits between banks, meaning any UPI-enabled app (PhonePe, GPay, Paytm) can talk to any bank account. NPCI acts as the settlement backbone. The whole thing runs 24/7 with no merchant discount rate on most transactions.

That last bit is the radical part. In most payment systems, the merchant pays 1–3% per swipe. UPI charges nothing for most P2P and many P2M transactions. This is a policy choice, not a technical constraint.

## Why Everyone Is Paying Attention

The World Bank has been studying UPI since 2021. The Reserve Bank of Australia spent a year looking at why their own New Payments Platform underperformed relative to what India built. The BIS published three separate papers on UPI's architecture.

Singapore's PayNow already links directly to UPI for cross-border remittances. The UAE, France, and Mauritius have active corridors in progress. These aren't courtesy agreements — they're countries recognizing that India has solved a distribution problem no one else has cracked at scale.

## The Part Nobody Talks About

UPI's real disruption isn't the technology. It's the data.

Every transaction creates a structured data trail: who paid whom, for what, at what time, from which location. NPCI and the government do not use this for surveillance in the way some critics fear — but it does power the credit underwriting layer that sits above UPI in the form of AA (Account Aggregator) and OCEN (Open Credit Enablement Network).

A vegetable seller who processes 200 transactions a day through UPI now has a verifiable transaction history that a bank can underwrite against. That's how you build formal credit access for people who've been outside the system for decades.

## What Comes Next

The logical endpoint is UPI becoming a global settlement rail — not unlike SWIFT, but faster, cheaper, and owned by a different set of stakeholders. India is betting that the country that exports payment infrastructure gains the kind of soft power the US gets from dollar dominance.

Whether that bet pays off depends on whether NPCI can internationalize governance without losing the policy flexibility that made UPI work domestically.

Worth watching.
